text: A rich tradition of older scholarship interpreted the Epistle of James as being addressed to the Jewish diaspora at large, regardless of their commitment to the Christian faith. While contemporary scholars have largely moved away from this perspective, I argue that the thesis warrants renewed consideration. Indeed, such a reading provides a framework for understanding several perplexing features of the text, including its conspicuous lack of explicit Christology, its deficiency in distinctive Christian sentiment, and its pervasive Jewish orientation. I propose that the author was a Jewish Christian who sought to maintain a viable presence within the synagogue and desired irenic relations with those who did not recognize Jesus as the Messiah. Consequently, the author was intentionally reticent regarding specific theological convictions for apologetic purposes—a tactical silence that finds clear precedents in other strands of ancient Christian literature.
